title: How to Add Memory in LangGraph AI Agents | Full Tutorial
summary: A tutorial demonstrating how to implement memory systems in AI agents using LangGraph. Covers short-term and long-term memory, checkpointing, thread-level state management, and practical coding examples using Python.
writeup: A step-by-step tutorial on how LangGraph handles agent memory, covering both short-term (thread-level) and long-term memory and the checkpointer mechanism that underlies both. The video walks through a complete Python implementation using LangGraph with a Groq-hosted Llama 3.3 70B model, showing how conversation state is persisted across turns and sessions. The central argument is that memory is the foundational requirement for any real-world AI agent, not model capability or multi-agent topology.
key_points: LangGraph distinguishes two memory scopes: short-term (in-memory, scoped to a single conversation session and lost on restart) and long-term (persisted across sessions and application restarts), managed through a component called the checkpointer.

The checkpointer functions as an automatic save mechanism that snapshots the full graph state after every node execution, enabling an agent to resume from exactly where it left off after a crash or session end rather than restarting from scratch.

Short-term memory is implemented by passing an InMemorySaver checkpointer to the StateGraph at compile time; the same thread_id in the config dict is what binds multiple turns into one continuous session context.

Long-term memory requires a persistent store (such as a database-backed checkpointer) so that state survives process restarts, and LangGraph's checkpointer abstraction allows swapping the storage backend without changing any graph logic.

State in LangGraph is a typed dictionary shared across all nodes in the graph; nodes read from it and write back to it, and the messages key accumulates the full conversation history using the add reducer operator.

The tutorial uses Llama 3.3 70B via Groq as the LLM backend, installed through the langchain-groq package, demonstrating that LangGraph's memory architecture is model-agnostic and works with any LangChain-compatible LLM.
